text: BONCHAMPS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Argentré. Pop. 1,167. The environs contain quarries of grey marble.
BONCHURCH, a parish and pleasing village in the isle of Wight, in Southamptonshire, 9 m. SE of Newport. Area 150 acres. Pop. 302.

BONCOURT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meuse, cant. and 3 m. NNW of Commercy, near the r. bank of the Meuse. Pop. 576. The environs afford excellent white wine, and contain some ironworks.—Also a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Bern, 5 m. NW of Porrentruy, on the r. bank of the Allein. Pop. 602. It contains a custom-house.
BOND, a county in the state of Illinois, U. S., comprising a superficial area of 360 sq. m.; drained by Shoal creek, a tributary of the Kaskasia. Pop. in 1840, 5,060. Its cap. is Greenville.

BONDENO, a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the deleg. and 10 m. WNW of Ferrara, at the confluence of the Panaro with the Po d'Argento, and at the entrance of the passes of the Apennines. Pop. 3,000.
BONDGATE, a township in the p. of Ripon, W. R. of Yorkshire. Area, with Aismunderby, 1,670 acres. Pop. 614.
BONDIO, or BENDIA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Galicia, 10 m. NNE of Lugo.
BONDO (CAPE), a promontory of Luzon, in the Asiatic archipelago, at the SW extremity of the island, in N lat. 13° 10′, and E long. 122° 32′.
BONDORF, a bailiwick and town of Baden, in the circle of the Danube, 15 m. WNW of Schaffhausen. Pop. of town 900.

BONDU, a state of W Africa, lying between the Senegal and the Gambia, and the meridians of 12° and 13° 30' W long. It is bounded on the NW by Futa-Toro; on the NE by Kajaaga; on the E by Bambuk and Satadu; on the S by Dentilla, Neola, and Wulli; and on the W by Wulli, the forest-wilderness of Simbani, and Futa-Toro. The river Faleme, flowing N by W to the l. bank of the Senegal, separates B. from Bambuk and Satadu; the Nerico, rising near the parallel of 14° N, flows SW through B. to the r. bank of the Gambia. Its greatest extent from E to W, and from N to S, does not exceed 90 m. A great part of the surface is covered with forests, and towards the E side the surface rises into hills, which are also covered with thick woods in some places, and with low stunted shrubs in other places. From these hills innumerable torrents descend in the rainy season to the Senegal on the N, and the Faleme on the E. The valleys, where cleared in the vicinity of the towns and villages, present a light, rich, fertile soil, producing cotton, indigo, grain, rice, water-melons, pumpkins, tobacco, red-pepper, and pistachio-nuts. The baobab or Adansonia digitata, the tamarind, the Rhamnus lotus, and several other species of fruit-bearing trees, are thickly scattered over the country. A species of corn called by the natives manio, the Holcus cornuus of botanists, is sown in November, and reaped in the early part of January. The pop. of B. are Fulahs, Mandingoes, and Serawullis. The former are the dominant tribe. They generally profess Mahommedanism; and there are schools in every town in which the reading and writing of Arabic is taught by a marabut. Fulah customs and manners predominate. The prevailing complexion of this nation in B. is a light copper colour, with a contour of face approaching nearer to the European than that of any other tribe of W Africa, the Moors excepted. The women, Major Gray assures us-in somewhat exaggerated terms probably-"might vie, in point of figure, with those of the most exquisitely fine form in Europe." They are neat in their persons and dress; always wear a veil thrown loosely over the head; and according to the same authority have a peculiarly majestic and graceful walk. A large portion of their dress is manufactured by themselves. The useful arts are held in higher esteem in B. than in any of the countries to the W. The cap. of B. when Park visited the country, was Fatteconda; but in 1820, when Gray passed through it, it was Bulibani, nearly in the centre of the state, in an extensive plain at the foot of a range of rocky hills. The town, which appeared to contain about 1,800 inhabitants, was surrounded with a clay wall, 10 ft. high, and 18 inches thick, pierced with loop-holes, and having salient angles at short intervals. The streets of the cap. were narrow, irregular, and dirty; and the houses or huts presented every variety of hut-architecture. At a short distance to the SW were the ruins of a town nearly as large as Bulibani, of which it had originally formed a part, but which had been destroyed by an inroad from Kaarta, provoked by the rapacity of the almamy of B., who freely avails himself of his Mahommedan commission to make marauding expeditions on the frontiers of the neighbouring states, to obtain slaves for the Golam market. Major Gray estimated the whole disposable force of B. at between 500 and 600 horsemen, chiefly Yolofs, and from 2,000 to 3,000 foot. The almamy's revenues are composed of a tenth of all agricultural produce; a tenth of the salt imported; a transit-duty on all European goods; an annual present from the Senegal company's vessels trading on the river; and heavy exactions upon all travellers passing through his territories. The chief merchants are Mandingoes and Serawullis, who carry on a considerable trade with Gedumah, and other Moorish territories, bartering their corn and blue calicoes for salt, which they exchange again in other districts for iron, shea-butter, and gold-dust.- Park.—Golberry.—Durand.—Gray.
BONDUES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Tourcoing, 4 m. N of Lille. Oil and beet-root sugar are manufactured here.

BONDY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine, cant. of Pantin, near the forest of the same name, 7 m. ENE of Paris. Pop. 2,385. It contains a fine country seat and park, and many handsome villas on the Canal de l'Ourcq. It has numerous flocks of sheep, of the merino breed.
BONE, a river of the island of Formosa, in the Chinese sea, which takes its rise in the central ridge of mountains, and falls into the Eastern sea in N lat. 24° 12'.
BONE. See BONA.
BONEF, a village of Belgium, in the prov. and 10 m. N of Namur, cant. of Huy, on the l. bank of the Mehaigne. It was formerly celebrated for its abbey.
BONEFRO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Sannio, district and 7 m. SSE of Larino, situated on the slope of a mountain. Pop. 3,500. It contains a fine chateau, a parish-church, and 4 alms-houses.
BO'NESS. See Borrowstounness.
BONETE (EL), a town of Spain, in the prov. of Murcia, in the district and 20 m. E of Chinchilla. BON
BONFELD, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Lower Neckar, bailiwick and 8 m. NW of Heilbroun. Pop. 1,153. It contains a castle, and a synagogue.

BONFOL, or BUMPFEL, a parish of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, 5 m. NE of Porrentruy, on the l. bank of the Halle. Pop. 1,207, Catholics. It is a place of pilgrimage, and possesses a considerable trade in pottery.
BONG, a territory in Upper Assam, on the N frontiers of the Birman territories, inhabited chiefly by Singfos. It is watered by the head-branches of the Irawadi.

BONGHIR, or Vanaghiri, a town and fortress of Hindostan, cap. of a small district of the same name, in the prov. and 25 m. ENE of Hyderabad. The district is bounded on the S by the Mussy, and is the best cultivated and most populous portion of the Nizam's territories. It contains a considerable number of small towns and villages.
BONGO, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Philippine group, near the SW coast of the island of Mindanao, in Illana bay, in N lat. 7° 15', and E long. 124° 15'.
BONHEYDEN, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Antwerp, 4 m. E of Mechlin. Pop. 1,257.

BONHILL, a parish and village in Dumbartonshire, at the lower extremity of Loch Lomond, and intersected by the Leven. There are numerous extensive bleachfields and cotton printing-works in this p. Pop. in 1801, 2,460; in 1841, 6,682, of whom 2,041 were in the v. of B.
BONHOLZ, or Banholz, a town of Bavaria, in the circle and near the l. bank of the Regen, 9 m. N of Ratisbon.
BONHOMME, or DIEDOLSHAUSEN, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Upper Rhine, cant. of La Poutroye. Pop. 1,461. It contains some ironworks and manufactories of implements of husbandry.

BONHOMME (COL DU), a gorge in the Sardinian Alps, between the provinces of Faucigny and Tarantaise, 14 m. SW of Mont Blanc, and 20 m. SE of Sallanche. The alt. of the point called La Croix du B. is 8,195 ft. above sea-level [Forbes]; but the passage of the Col des Fours is 850 ft. higher. [Ibid.] The view from the former part embraces the mountains of the Upper Isère stretching towards Mont Cenis, and conspicuous amongst them the Aiguille de la Vanoise or Chaffe-Quarre, a snow-clad pyramidal summit in the Grecian Alps, alt. 12,674 ft. Immediately below the spectator is the deep valley of Bonneval. Mont Blanc is not visible from this point; but from the Col des Fours it appears rising above the Col de la Seigne. Two peaked rocks near the lower col, known as the Col de la Gauche, bearing the name of B. and La Femme de B., gave name to this pass. It requires 4 or 5 hours to cross from the chalets of Nant Bourant, 31 hours above St. Gervais, to the chalets of Chapiu, 3 hours from St. Maurice in the Tarentaise.

BONI, a federal state in the island of Celebes, stretching along the head and W shore of the large bay or gulf of Sewa, sometimes called the bay of B., on the S coast of the island. It consists of eight petty states, each governed by its own hereditary arung or chief, while the general government is vested in one of the number elected by the rest. The chiefs also choose the tumilabang or first minister, by whom justice is administered; and the arunga, or head of the federal union, can decide on no public measures affecting the revenue, or the external relations of the state, without the consent of his arnugpitu [Crawfurd], orang-pitu [Raffles], or council of seven lords. Crawfurd says all his public documents run thus: "We, the king, and the people of B., decree, resolve, &c." The arung are themselves elected. Their offices indeed are hereditary in certain families, but the council chooses their successor, and will even remove an obnoxious individual, and proceed to a new election in his life-time. Independent of their deliberative functions, the arnug-pitu hold also various executive offices in the federal state. Any individual of the privileged families, even-as not unfrequently happens—a woman, or an infant, is eligible to be raised either to the rank of arung or of arunga. When a woman, or a minor, is raised to the latter office, the constitution provides for them a guardian, called madangrang, i.e. 'Prop,' or 'Support.' An elective monarchy, limited by an aristocracy generally hereditary, and exercising feudal authority over the minor chiefs and pop., is a constitution of civil society which, however common in Europe, is perhaps without parallel in any other part of Asia; but the whole of the Bugi states in Celebes appear to be constituted on this peculiar principle. A report made in 1824 by the Dutch residents in Celebes, estimated the pop. of the B. federacy at 200,000, chiefly Bughis or Bugis. The Chinrana, a large river, rising in the Wadji or Waju country, in a great fresh water lake called Tapara-karaja, in the SW limb of Celebes, crosses the B. territory, and discharges itself by several mouths into Sewa bay. It is navigable a considerable distance by ships of 400 tons burthen; and for the largest native craft to the lake itself. The natives of B. manufacture and even largely export a strong cotton cloth; they also collect and export to the extremities of the archipelago, gold dust, nutmegs, edible birds' nests, camphor, gums, and tortoise-shell; and import opium, European and Indian cotton stuffs, broadcloth, trepang, iron, and tobacco. A great portion of the produce collected by them is carried to Singapore, where it is exchanged for calicoes, iron, muskets, gunpowder, and other articles of British and Indian manufacture. See article Bugis.—The king of the Macassars is represented to have been converted to Mahommedanism by a preacher from Sumatra in the beginning of the 17th cent., and to have imposed his new faith on the people of B. by force of arms about the year 1640. In 1663 Raja Palaka of B. visited Batavia; and in 1666 he co-operated with the Dutch against the native states on the coast of Sumatra: from this period, the importance of B. appears to have advanced. In 1814, the British government on Java found it necessary to chastise the rajah of B., and an expedition under General Nightingale was despatched against the town of B., which was captured. With the general restoration of colonies in 1816, the Dutch influence became again paramount in B. Stavorinus has given a sketch of the history of B. with some curious details of its revolutions, and of the personal character of its kings; but has added little to our knowledge of the country and its productions.-The bay of B.-also called Buggen Bay-is a deep gulf, extending nearly 180 m. in length from N to S, between the parallels of 2° 38' and 3° 30' S lat., by about 50 m. in breadth, between the two great southern arms or peninsulas of this singularly shaped island. It is
BONI, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Kostroma, on the Kostroma river, 50 m. NNE of Kostroma.
BONICES, a village of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. of Soria, 12 m. S of Soria, on the head stream of the Douro.

BONIFACIO, a commune and town in the island of Corsica, cant. of Ajaccio, and arrond. of Sartène. The town is finely situated on a small rocky peninsula on the S coast of the island, 42 m. SE of Ajaccio, in BON the Bocche di B., or Strait of B., a narrow channel between Corsica and Sardinia, about 6 m. in average breadth, but narrowing in one point to 3 m. B. is neatly built, and well-fortified, and contains several handsome churches of Pisan architecture. Pop. 3,000. Its harbour is a fine oval creek about 1 m. in length, and 70 fath. in breadth, affording anchorage in 5 or 6 fath. Some trade in wine, cheese, oil, and coral, is conducted from it.—This town is supposed to be the most ancient in the island. It was founded by Boniface, Count of Corsica, a native of Pisa, in 833; and had peculiar privileges accorded it while under Genoese dominion.
BONIFATI, a commune and town of Naples, in Calabria Citra, in the district of Paola, 3 m. SSE of Belvedere, and 25 m. NNW of Cosenza. Pop. 2,670. Silk is cultivated in the environs.
BONIFAZIO (SAN), a town of Venetian-Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, deleg. and 15 m. E of Verona. Pop. 2,800.
BONILA, a town of Albania, 4 m. SW of Joanina, on the W side of the lake of Joanina.
BONILLA-DE-LA-SIERRA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 35 m. WNW of Avila, on the Merderos. Pop. 1,700.
BONILLO, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 45 m. WNW of Chinchilla. Pop. 2,000.

BONIN ISLANDS, a group in the N Pacific, extending from the parallel of 27° 44′ 35" N. southward, and between 143° and 144° E long. To the N cluster, which consists of small islands and pointed rocks, Captain Beechey-who visited and explored in part this group in 1827—gave the name of Parry's group. The middle cluster consists of three islands, of which the S one, Peel's island 43 m. in length, is the largest; the N island of this cluster, Captain Beechey named Stapleton, and the centre Buckland. These three islands are divided by very narrow channels. The S cluster, which was visited by Mr. Coffin in 1823, has been named Baily's group. There is great depth of water around, and strong currents set between these islands. These islands are of volcanic formation; steep and high, and thickly wooded down to the shore. Almost every valley has a stream of water, and the vegetation is luxuriant. The Areca oleracea and fan-palm are conspicuous amongst the larger trees. Camphor and spice trees are said to abound. The rocky shores are the resort of crayfish and other shell-fish; and the shores swarm with sharks and green turtle. The only wild animal of the mammalia class which Captain Beechey's party saw upon these islands was, as they supposed, the vampire-bat; but Dr. Ruschenberger pronounced it a species of ptyropus, or flying fox. Of birds, they saw herons, plovers, rails, snipes, wood-pigeons, and the common crow. In June 1830, a small company of adventurers sailed from the Sandwich islands, accompanied by several of the natives male and female, and began the settlement of Peel's island. To Dr. Ruschenberger-who visited the B. islands in 1836this little community seemed rapidly relapsing into savage life. Instead of a mild and benignant climate, every year had brought typhoons and earthquakes, which had devastated their plantations; and their labour had been severe in clearing small patches of nearly on the equatorial line, and in E long. 131° 10'. soil from the close jungle. Their settlement, called near Port St. George, an oval bay about 11 m. long, and 3 m. wide, in N lat. 27° 6′ 30″, E long. 142° 16′ [Chinese Repository, 1835]. Their crops consisted of melons, pumpkins, yams, sweet potatoes, taro or Arum esculentum, sugar-cane, and Indian corn. Hogs, goats and dogs are numerous. Captain Beechey thinks that these islands are the YSLAS DEL ARZOBISPO of Spanish navigators; and that the Bonin-sima, or Mou-nin-sima, [i. e. 'Desert isles,'] of the Japanese, if they have existence at all, must be another group. Beechey's Voyage.—Ruschenberger's Voyage.
BONINGALE, or Boninghall, a parish in the co. of Salop, in Brimstree hund. Area 690 acres. Pop. 184.
BONIN-SIMA. See BONIN.
BONISTALLO, a parish of Tuscany, in the district of Florence, 3 m. E of Carmignano, on the Ombrone-Pistojese. Pop. 1,425. It contains the grand ducal villa of Poggio-a-Cajano.
BONITO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Principato Ultra, dist. and 7 m. SW of Ariano. Pop. 2,560. It contains several churches, 2 convents, and an hospital.

BONJEM, a town of N Africa, in the district of Fezzan, on the frontiers and 200 m. SE of Tripoli, and 320 m. NNE of Murzuk. It contains the remains of a Roman fortress of the time of Septimus Severus, now almost entirely buried in the sand.
BONLAHY, a village in the p. of Granard, co. Longford. Pop. 238.
BONMAHON, a village in the p. of Ballylaneen, 4 m. SSE of Kilmacthomas, in co. Waterford, at the mouth of the Mahon river. Pop. 1,771.

BONN, a neat town of the Prussian states, in the circle of Cologne, prov. of the Rhine, on the l. bank of the Rhine, in N lat. 50° 44', E long. 9° 44'. It was formerly in the French dep. of the Rhine and Moselle. It lies in an agreeable and fertile country, and is the first post-station from Cologne to Mayence. From Nieder Wessling to B., the road gradually approaches the river, from which it had at first receded, and on entering the town it nearly touches its margin. It is likewise connected with the former city by a railway 18 m. in length. The buildings are regular and good; but the views on the Rhine are overpowered by the size of the river. [H. W. Williams.] The pop. at the end of last century was 11,000, one-half of whom were artists and mechanics, and the rest attached to the court of the elector of Cologne, which had been held here since 1268; but after the extinction of the electoral dignity, the pop. of B. rapidly diminished, and was found, in 1801, to amount to only 8,837. The institution of the university gave it a new impulse, and in 1837 it amounted to 13,871. The trade carried on here is chiefly in the hands of Jews, who are about 500 in number. The manufactures of the place consist of cotton and silk stuffs, tobacco, soap, and vitriol. An active commerce is carried on by the river in grain, oleaginous seeds, wine, and lead. Bonn has four parish-churches; that of St. Martin is built in the form of a Roman rotunda. The university of B. was founded by the king of Prussia in October, 1818. The king gave the castles of B. and Poppelsdorf, with their appurtenances, and a revenue of 80,000 thalers, to the university, which consists of five faculties, two of which are theological to accommodate Protestant and Catholic students; the other three are jurisprudence, medicine, and philosophy. The university occupies a chateau formerly the electoral residence between the Poppelsdorf allee, and the public library. The surgical, medical, and clerical hospitals, are all contained within the building. In the castle of Poppelsdorf, which is joined to the university by a beautiful avenue of chestnut-trees nearly 1 m. in length, are placed the cabinets of natural history, which are very rich in zoological and mineralogical specimens, and contained in all, in 1834, 92,561 subjects. The botanical garden extends to 20 acres. Niebuhr, W. Schlegel, Freytag, Bopp, and Lessen, have filled chairs in this university. The university is generally attended by from 600 to 1,000 students. Its library contains about 70,000 vols.—B. BON is likewise the seat of an agricultural college and an experimental farm. - This town, the Bona, or Bonna ad Rhenum, of the Romans, was destroyed in the 4th cent., and rebuilt by the emperor Julian. The Huns, Franks, Saxons, and Normans, in succession captured it. It formerly ranked among the fortresses of the Rhine, and suffered severely from bombardment, when garrisoned by the French in 1673, 1689, and 1703; on the latter occasion it was taken by Marlborough. In 1717 the greater part of the fortifications were demolished, in order to make way for the magnificent electoral palace, which was completed some years afterwards. B. was likewise the seat of the electoral government and courts-of-justice. Its academy was instituted in 1777, and received the title and privileges of an university in 1786; but was dissolved by the French.—The Rhine, opposite B., has an annual mean breadth of 1,200 ft., and mean depth of 15 ft., with a mean velocity of 2 m. per hour. A flying bridge or boat, moored to anchors in the middle of the stream, and large enough to take 700 or 800 people across at a time, is used to cross the river. It swings from one side to another according to the direction of the helm, the current being the only impelling force.—The environs of B. are interesting to English geologists as being the nearest point where volcanic phenomena, approaching in character to those of modern times, can be seen. In the immediate neighbourhood of the Siebengebirge ['Seven mountains'] the strata have been thrown up in all directions by the eruption of volcanic matter; and the main body of the Siebengebirge is composed of trachyte, basalt, and other volcanic rocks. On the opposite side of the Rhine, the Rodderberg, composed of cinders and scorified rocks, presents an extinct volcano, with a crater about 1 m. in diam., and 100 ft. deep. [Horner, in Trans. of Geol. Soc., 1833.]—The circle of B., one of the 11 subdivisions of the circle of Cologne, has an area of about 105 sq. m., and contains 1 town, and 58 villages. Pop. in 1831, 42,447.
BONN, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. and 5 m. N of Freyburg, on the r. bank of the Saane. It is celebrated for its sulphureous springs and baths.
BONNAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Vienne, cant. of Ambazac. Pop. 1,011.

BONNAT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Creuse, arrond. of Guéret. The cant. comprises 12 com., and contained, in 1831. a pop. of 13,677. The town is 13 m. N of Gueret. Pop. 2,702.

BONNE, a town of Savoy, in the prov. of Caronge, on the r. bank of the Menoge, 7 m. NW of Bonneville, and 9 m. ESE of Geneva. Pop. 560. It was formerly a place of considerable importance, defended by a castle of great strength.
BONNE BAY, an indentation of the W coast of Newfoundland, in N lat. 49° 30′, W long. 58°.
BONNE CHAIRE, a river of Upper Canada, which falls into Lake Chat, on the NE confines of M'Nabb township, in Bathurst co.
BONNE FEMME, a town of Howard co., in the state of Missouri, U. S., Pop. 988.
BONNE HOMME, a town of St. Genevieve co., in the state of Missouri, U.S. Pop. 436.—Also a town in St. Louis co., in Missouri. Pop. 2,516.
BONNELLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Dourdan. Pop. 502.
BONNEMAIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Combourg. Pop. 1,663.
BONNES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente, cant. of Aubeterre. Pop. 1,065.—Also a commune in the dep. of Vienne, cant. of St. Julienl'Ars. Pop. 1,381.
BONNET, a rivulet in co. Leitrim, rising in the Rossclogher mountains, and flowing SSW to Dromahaire, and then NNW to the head of Lough Gilly, into which it falls after a course of 13 or 14 m.
BONNET CARRE, a town, the cap. of St. John Baptist's parish, in the state of Louisiana, U. S., 38 m. W of New Orleans, on a remarkable bend of the Mississippi of the same name.

BONNET (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Upper Alps, arrond. of Gap. The cant. comprises 20 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 11,612. The town is situated on the Drac, 10 m. N of Gap, at an alt. of 2,398 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,800.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Lower Charente, cant. of Mirabeau, on the r. bank of the Gironde. Pop. 1,555. It possesses a small port.—Also a commune in the dep. of Upper Vienne, in the cant. and 7 m. NW of Bellac, on the Gartempe. Pop. 1,455. It affords considerable quantities of wine.
BONNET-LE-BOURG (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of St. Germain-l'Herm. Pop. 996.
BONNET-EN-BRESSE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Pierre. Pop. 1,214.
BONNET-DES-BRUYE'RES (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Rhone, cant. of Monsol, 27 m. NW of Villefranche. Pop. 1,486.
BONNET-LE-CHASTEL (SAINT), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of St. Germain-l'Herm, near the Dolore, 10 m. SW of Ambert. Pop. 1,520.

BONNET-LE-CHATEAU (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Loire, arrond. of Montbrison.—The cant. comprises 10 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 15,276.—The town is situated on a hill 15 m. S of Montbrison, and contains manufactories of lace and locks. Timber forms its chief article of commerce. B. is of great antiquity, and was formerly fortified. Pop. 2,169.
BONNET-LE-COURROUX (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire, cant. of St.-George'sen-Couzan. Pop. 1,833.
BONNET-DE-CRAY (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Semur. Pop. 1,055.
BONNET-LE-DESERT (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of Cerilly, on the I. bank of the Sologne, 36 m. W of Moulins. Pop. 890. It possesses important iron-works.
BONNET-ELVERT (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Correze, cant. of Argentat. Pop. 1,252.

BONNET-DE-JOUX (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Saone-et- Loire.—The cant. comprises 7 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 6,541.—The town, 10 m. NE of Charolles, has a pop. of 1,430. It has a considerable trade in thread; and in the environs are quarries of freestone.
BONNET-PRES-ORCIVAL (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Rochefort. Pop. 1,189.
BONNET-DES-QUARTS (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire, cant. of Riom. Pop. 1,494.

BONNET-LA-RIVIERE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Correze, cant. of Juillac. Pop. 1,018.—Also a commune in the dep. of Upper Vienne, cant. of Pierre-Buffiere, 16 m. SE of Limoges. Pop. 1,584. It contains iron-mines and works.
BONNET-DE-ROCHEFORT (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of Gannat. Pop. 1,333.
BONNET-LE-TRONCY (SAINT), a commune of BON France, in the dep. of Rhone, cant. of St. Nizierd'Azergues. Pop. 1,498.

BONNE'TABLE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, arrond. and 13 m. S of Mamers, on the Dive, in the vicinity of an extensive forest. Pop. 5,803. It contains an ancient castle; and has manufactories of cotton fabrics, and tile-works. Cattle fairs are held here eight times a-year.
BONNEUIL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Gonesse, 15 m. from Pontoise. Pop. 443. It contains manufactories of machinery.
BONNEUIL-LES-EAUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Oise, cant. of Breteuil, 22 m. from Clermont. Pop. 1,273.
BONNEUIL-MATOURS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vienne, cant. of Vonneuil. Pop. 1,305.

BONNEVAL, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Eure-et-Loir, arrond. of Châteaudun.-The cant. comprises 20 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 12,334.—The town is situated in a fertile valley, 10 m. NNE of Chateaudun, on the Loir, which is here crossed by eight bridges. Pop. 2,432. B. was formerly fortified, and contained an abbey. Its streets are broad, and its church is adorned with a lofty spire. Cotton and woollen fabrics form the chief articles of manufacture. There are also cotton spinning and printing-mills, tanneries, and a fuller's-mill. A cattle fair is held at B. once a-year.
BONNEVEAU, a village of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of St. Jean-de-Bournay, 15 m. ESE of Vienne. It has an extensive glass-work.

BONNEVILLE, a town of Savoy, in the prov. of Faucigny, on the r. bank of the Arve, at an alt. of 1,427 ft. above sea-level, 14 m. ESE of Geneva, and 48 m. NNE of Chambery. Pop. 1,500. It is of considerable antiquity, is well-built, and has a royal college. In the environs are the ruins of an old castle, and quarries of brown freestone.
BONNEVILLE-LES-BOUCHOUX. See Bou- CHOUX.
BONNEVILLE-LA-LOUVET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. of Blangy. Pop. 1, 370.

BONNIE'RES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Auxy-le-Château. Pop. 1,107.—Also a canton and commune in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, arrond. of Mantes. The cant. comprises 27 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 12,401. The com. is on the l. bank of the Seine, 9 m. WNW of Mantes, and contains quarries of freestone.
BONNIEUX, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, arrond. of Apt. The cant. comprises 6 com., and contained a pop. of 6,733 in 1831.—The com. is 6 m. SW of Apt. Pop. 2,560.

BONNIGHEIM, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Neckar, 6 m. NNW of Besigheim, and 11 m. NNW of Ludwigburg, near the Neckar, at the foot of the Michelberg, at an alt. of 712 ft. above sealevel. Pop. 2,994. It is of considerable antiquity; and contains an ancient royal castle, and a church remarkable for the magnificence of its high altar. Numerous Roman remains exist in the environs.
BONNINGTON, a parish in Kent, 6½ m. SE by S of Ashford. Area 4,310 acres. Pop. 155.
BONNUT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lower Pyrenees, cant. of Orthez. Pop. 1,202.

BONNY, a large river falling into the bight of Biafra, on the W coast of Africa, by an estuary 8 m. in breadth, consisting of the mouth of the B. to the E, and of the New Calabar river to the W. Foche point, the W point of this double estuary, is in N lat. 4° 22′ 40", E long. 7°; and Rough corner, the E point, is in N lat. 4° 23′ 40″, E long. 7°. There is a channel on each side of the estuary, into the respective rivers; and the central part is occupied by a large sand-bank, having only 3 ft. water at several points. Either channel can be entered at any time of tide by vessels drawing from 12 to 18 ft. water. A mile above Rough corner is Jew-Jew point; and about 3 m. higher, on the same side, is the town of B. The water deepens within the bar to about 10 fath.; and for the first 7 or 8 m., the breadth is nearly 4 m. The shores are covered with mangroves, and the whole country appears swampy about the lower part of the B. Little has yet been reported of the course of the river above the town of B. It seems to come from the NW; and may probably be found to be a deltoid branch of the Niger, which opposite to Aboh puts off a branch from its l. bank, which is said to lead to the town of Bonny. This river was at one period the chief resort of the slavers. When Captain Adams visited it about 30 years ago, upwards of 20,000 slaves, he was informed, were annually sold by the people of B., of whom nearly four-fifths, he was told, were natives of Heebo [Iboh, or Eboe?] Fairs, where the slaves of the Heebo nation were obtained, were held every five or six weeks at several villages on the banks of the rivers and creeks in the interior, whither the traders of B. resorted to purchase them, in large canoes capable of carrying 120 persons. These canoes generally arrived on the expiration of the sixth day from their departure, bringing with them from 1,500 to 2,000 slaves. [Adam's Remarks, 1823.] Notwithstanding the vigilance of British cruisers it is thought that slaves to the number of 2,000 are still annually exported from this river. With the diminution of the slave trade in the bight of Biafra, the trade in palm-oil has increased. Three-fourths of the palm-oil imported into Liverpool are from the B. The oil is brought down from the interior, a distance of about 150 m., and sold to the B. traders in small quantities, from whom it is purchased by the English traders, and paid for in gunpowder, guns, cutlasses, lead, iron bars, boilers, tobacco, rum, and Manchester goods. The importation of this article in 1816, from the coast of Africa, did not exceed £23,831 in value; in 1846 it amounted to 360,452 cwts. Ivory is also exported in large quantities. Yet the people of B., who have the most of this trade in their hands, are one of the most barbarous, dishonest, cruel, and superstitious races in all this benighted part of Africa. The river is unhealthy from the month of September to June. [Madden's Report.]—The town of B. is situated on the l. point of a large and deep creek which, it is said, communicates with the Andony river 15 m. to the E. The huts have some resemblance to an English cottage, and are constructed of stakes plastered with mud, with a roof composed of palm-leaves. Its chief exercises a controlling influence over the trade of the river; and has several war-canoes, some of which exceed 70 ft. in length, and are mounted with guns of small calibre. [Owen.]
BONNY, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Loiret, cant. of Briare, 15 m. SE of Gien, on the r. bank of the Loire. Pop. 1,608.
BONNY, a small stream in Stirlingshire, which takes its rise in the p. of Kilsyth, and, flowing E, falls into the Carron below Dunipace.
BONNYRIG, a village in the p. of Cockpen, Mid Lothian, 7 m. S of Edinburgh. Pop. 650.
BONNYBRIDGE, a village in the p. of Falkirk, in Stirlingshire, 4 m. from Falkirk, on the turnpike road to Glasgow. Pop. 100.

BONO, a village of the island of Sardinia, prov. of Nuoro, on the r. bank of the Tirsi, 23 m. SE of Sassari. Pop. 2,100. BOO pelago, in Pitt's Passage, near the NW extremity of the island of Ceram, in S lat. 3°, and E long. 128°. It is about 25 m. in circumf., and abounds with cocoe and ebony trees, and is fertile in rice.
BONORVA, a town of the island of Sardinia, in the prov. of Alghero, 9 m. ENE of Bosa, at the foot of a mountain. Pop. 4,274. It contains two convents.
BONPUR. See BUNPUR.
BONS, or Bans, a town of Savoy, in the prov. of Genevois, on the l. bank of the Rhone, 19 m. NW of Annecy.
BONSALL, a parish in Derbyshire, 2 m. SW of Matlock, on the W bank of the Derwent. Area 2,750 acres. Pop. 1,496.
BONSECOURS. See Blosseville-Bonsecours.

BONSECOURS, a seignory of Lower Canada, in the co. and par. of L'Islet, extending from the St. Lawrence to the frontier of the United States; and bounded on the NE by the seignory of Islet; and on the SW by that of Vincelet. It is intersected by several ridges of hills. With the exception of the Bras St. Nicholas, it contains no streams of importance; but is to a great extent well-cultivated, and its upper district affords excellent timber. It is crossed in its entire extent by an excellent government road.—Also a seignory on the S bank of the St. Lawrence, in the par. of St. Antoine, co. of Lotbiniere, between the seignory of Pilly, or St. Antoine, and that of St. Croix. It is 6 m. in length; and 53 m. in breadth. It slopes considerably towards the St. Lawrence, and is destitute of streams. It affords, however, large quantities of excellent timber. - Also a seignory in the co. of Richlieu, between the seignory of Sorel and the Yamaska river. Its soil possesses little fertility, and is still to a great extent covered with natural wood.
BONSTETTEN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 5 m. SW of Zurich, in the amt of Knonau. Pop. 1,034. Peats are extensively dug in the environs.

BONTAIN, or BONTHAIN, a town in the island of Celebes, in the Asiatic archipelago, in a district and on a bay of the same name, on the S coast, 42 m. SE of Macassar, in S lat. 5° 33', and E long. 120°. In the vicinity of the town is a small palisadoed fort. The district is fertile, producing rice, fruit, timber, and abounds with fowls, hogs, &c. It formerly belonged to the rajah of Macassar; but is now subject to Holland. The bay, which is capacious, is wellsheltered, and affords good anchorage. The tides are extremely irregular, but rarely exceed 6 ft. in rise.
BONVILSTON, a parish in Glamorganshire, 8 m. W by S of Cardiff. Pop. 282.

BONVOULOIR, a group of islets in the Lousiade archipelago, in the S Pacific, to the SE of New Guinea, in S lat. 10° 20′, and E long. 151° 55′. The inhabitants are Negroes, have woolly hair and are of middle stature, and are said to be cannibals. This group was discovered by Entrecasteaux.
BONWICK, a township in the p. of Skipsea, in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 11 m. ESE of Great Driffield. Area 920 acres. Pop. 29.

BOOBY ISLAND, an islet off the SE end of St. Kitt's, in the Antilles, in N lat. 17° 14'.-Also an islet off the W coast of Jamaica, in N lat. 18° 20'.— Also an islet off the N coast of Australia, 9° 45' E of Port Essington, at which ships are in the habit of leaving letters for transmission by any vessel proceeding in the required direction. It is 1 m. in diam., and about 30 ft. high. Its W point is in S lat. 10° 36′ 42″, E long. 141° 57′ 45″. Variation 7° E. [Stokes.]
BOODROOM. See BUDRUN.
BOOKHAM (GREAT and LITTLE), two parishes
BONOA, or Bonaa, an island of the Asiatic archi- in Surrey, the former 21 m., the latter 3 m., SW of B00 Leatherhead. Area of Great B., 3,170 acres. Pop. 963. Area of Little B., 950 acres. Pop. 237.
BOOL, a town of Hungary, in the com. of Baranya, 16 m. SE by S of Funfkirchen.
BOOLEY, a township in the p. of Stanton-upon- Hineheath, in Salop, 5 m. SE of Wem. Pop. 117.
BOOM, a town of Belgium, in the prov. of Antwerp, on the r. bank of the Rupel, 11 m. S of Antwerp. Pop. 6,223. It contains salt-refineries, tanyards, brick and tile kilns, and rope-walks.
BOOLYGLAN, a village in the p. of Aghaviller, co. Kilkenny. Pop. 216.
BOON ISLAND, an island off the coast of Maine, U. S., between York river and Cape Neddock. On its N end is a lighthouse, with a fixed light 70 ft. high, in N lat. 43° 7½, W long. 70° 27½.

BOONE, a county in the extreme N part of the state of Kentucky, U.S. Area 300 sq. m. Its cap. is Burlington. Pop. 10,034.—Also a co. in the central part of the state of Iowa. Area 408 sq. m. Its cap. is Lebanon. Pop. 8,121.—Also a co. in the N part of the state of Illinois, watered by the Kishwaukee. Its cap. is Belvidere. Pop. 1,705.—Also a co. in the central part of the state of Missouri, watered by Rock and Cedar creeks. Area 690 sq. m. Its cap. is Columbia. Pop. 13,561.—Also a township in Warrick co., in Iowa. Pop. 1,722.— Also a township in Harrison co., in Iowa. Pop. 2,058. -Also a township in Franklin co., in Missouri. Pop. 669.
BOONESBOROUGH, a village in Washington co., in the state of Maryland, U. S., 91 m. SSW of Annapolis.—Also a v. in Madison co., Kentucky, 53 m. SSW of Frankfort.
BOONE LICK, a township in Howard co., in Missouri, U. S.
BOONE'S MILLS, a township in Franklin co., in the state of Virginia, U. S., 175 m. SW by W of Richmond.

BOONEVILLE, a township in Oneida co., in the state of New York, U. S., 28 m. N of Utica. It is drained by Black river, and the head-waters of the Mohawk. Pop. 5,516.—Also the cap. of Scott co., in Arkansas.—Also the cap. of Warwick co., in Iowa, 173 m. SSW of Indianapolis. Pop. 240.—Also the cap. of Cooper co., in Missouri, 50 m. NW of Jefferson, on the S bank of the Missouri. Pop. 2,581.

BOOS, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Schwaben- Neuburg, 9 m. from Babenhausen. Pop. 600.—Also a com. and cant. of France, in the dep. of Seine- Inferieure, arrond. of Rouen. The cant. comprises 16 com., with a pop. in 1831 of 11,552. The com. is 8 m. SE of Rouen. Pop. 927.
BOOSEMPRA, or Bosempra, a river of Guinea, sometimes known as St. John's RIVER, or the Cha-

MAH, forming the W boundary of the Fantee territory, now incorporated with Ashantee. It is navigable by small craft for about 50 or 60 m., to a place where navigation is impeded by a cataract. The Dutch factory of St. Sebastian stands at its mouth, in about 2° 10' W long.
BOOTAN. See BUTAN.
BOOTERSTOWN, a parish and v. in co. Dublin. Area of p. 541 acres. Pop. 3,318.—The v., which is nearly contiguous with Williamstown, is 34 m. from Dublin.
BOOTH'S ISLANDS, a group lying to the W of Cape Parry, in the Arctic sea, at the entrance of Franklin bay, in N lat. 70° 5', W long. 127° 50'.
BOOTH'S SOUND, an inlet of Baffin's bay, on the W coast of Greenland, in N lat. 76° 49'.

BOOTHBAY, a township in Lincoln co., in the state of Maine, U. S., 40 m. S by E of Augusta, between the Sheepscot and Damariscotta rivers. It has an excellent harbour; and a considerable pro- BOO portion of the pop. are employed in the fisheries. Pop. 2,631.
BOOTHBY, a parish in Lincolnshire, 10 m. NW by N of Sleaford. Area 2,850 acres. Pop. 214.
BOOTHBY PAGNELL, a parish in Lincolnshire, 41 m. NNW of Corby. Area 2,560 acres. Pop. 132.

BOOTHIA FELIX, a large peninsular projection at the NE angle of the American continent, discovered by Captain, now Sir John Ross, in 1830, and named by him in honour of Felix Booth, Esq., a patriotic citizen of London, to whose public spirit and rare munificence the expedition was entirely due. Captain Ross, proceeding down Barrow's strait, entered Regent's inlet, and penetrated on the W side of that channel as far as Cape Garry, the last point which Sir Edward Parry had been able to attain. He then made progress to the southward, running down an irregular and newly discovered line of coast to below the latitude of 70°, where he was beset with ice and detained three seasons. During this detention, expeditions were made under the command both of Captain Ross, and his nephew, as far as to Point Franklin, which, in the apprehension of these navigators, defined the continental coast-line between the latitudes of 72° 30′ and 69° N, and under longitudes lying between 89° and 99° W, and left a space of only 222 m. between Point Franklin of Ross, and Point Turnagain of Franklin, unexplored to the E, with a space of about 150 m. to the W; and established, in regard to the question of a NW passage, that there is none through Prince Regent's inlet, or to the S of 74° N. To the large peninsula along which they had coasted, and the whole E side of which, with about 150 m. of the SW side, they had explored, they gave the name of Boothia Felix; and on the isthmus by which they supposed it to be joined to the great body of the American continent, they bestowed the name of the isthmus of Boothia, which, as they conceived, formed the only barrier between the deep gulf on the E, to which they gave the name of the gulf of B., and the gulf on the W, which they had ascertained to be an inlet of King William's sea. The existence of this dividing isthmus, however, was strongly disputed by several eminent geographers. Sir John Barrow, in particular, whose authority in Arctic geography was deservedly eminent, insisted that the newly discovered land by Captain Ross must be insular. The protracted absence of the Rosses had meanwhile led to an overland expedition under Captain Back. The furthest point reached by Back was the estuary of the river which has been named after him, which, according to his observations, runs on the same meridian, and 2½° of lat. to the S of the parallel on which the observations of Ross placed his isthmus. Back's expedition to Repulse bay, whence he was to have proceeded overland to what Ross called the gulf of Boothia,—surveying its southern shore, connecting the survey with Ross's, then crossing the isthmus, and proceeding along the shore of King William's sea until he reached Point Turnagain, was baffled by causes which reflect no discredit on that able seaman. Messrs. Dease and Simpson, in 1839, traced the coast from Cape Turnagain to Back's estuary, and thence eastward to a point which they named Cape Selkirk, and to which they assigned the latitude of 68° 28' 23" N, and the long. of 94° 14' W. At this, their furthest east, Messrs. Dease and Simpson discerned several small and apparently disconnected portions of high land to the N, which they supposed to be islands; while to the NE another fragment of high blue land was visible, which they conjectured to be "one of the SE promontories of Boothia," and designated Cape Sir John Ross. In July 1846, an expedition, under the command of Dr. John Rae, left Fort Churchill, in Hudson's bay, for the purpose of tracing the coast of America between Dease and Simpson's farthest and the strait of the Fury and Hecla, on the N of Melville peninsula, to which Parry had penetrated in his expedition of 1821-2. The connexion of these two points would of course determine the insular or peninsular nature of Sir John Ross's discoveries. This expedition struck the coast in N lat. 67° 13', W long. by account 87° 30', and wintered on Melville peninsula, at Fort Hope, in N lat. 66° 32′ 16", W long. 86° 55′ 51". In the month of April 1847 they moved their encampment to Keith bay, in N lat. 68° 17', W long. 88° 22', whence on the 17th of that month Dr. Rae set out in company with two men for the purpose of following the coast to some point surveyed by Sir John Ross, as he now felt confident that that veteran discoverer was correct in his opinion as to B. Felix being part of the American continent. They directed their course to the furthest visible land which bore NW (true). The weather was beautiful but cold, and the ice being smooth, a brisk walk of 17 m. brought them to Cape Berens, in N lat. 69° 4′ 12", and W long. 90° 35'. The shore still trended to the NW, and they had not travelled more than 4 leagues on the 18th, when the coast took a sharp turn to the E; and as they were now near the lat. and long. of Lord Mayor's bay of Sir John Ross, they struck across land nearly in a N direction, and, at noon, when passing over a considerable lake, the lat. 69° 26' 1" N was observed. Advancing 3 m. beyond this they reached another lake, and, as there was yet no appearance of the sea, "a walk of 20 minutes," continues Dr. Rae, "brought me to an inlet not more than 1 m. wide: this I traced to the westward for 3 m., when my course was again obstructed by land. Ascending some high rocks, from which a good view could be obtained, I thought I could distinguish rough ice in the desired direction. With renewed hopes I set out at a rapid pace, plunging among deep snow, scrambling over rocks and through rough ice, until I gained some rising ground close to the beach. From the spot where I now stood, as far as the eye could see to the NW, lay a large extent of ice-covered sea, studded with innumerable islands. Lord Mayor's bay was before me, and the islands were those named by Sir John Ross 'the Sons of the Clergy of the Church of Scotland.' The isthmus which connects the land to the N with B. Felix, is only 1 m. broad; and, to judge by the number of stone marks set up on it, appears to be a favourite resort of the natives. Its latitude is 69° 31' N; long., by account, 91° 29′ 30" W. With a grateful heart to Him who had thus brought our journey so far to a successful termination, I began to retrace my steps towards my companions, and at a late hour reached the snow hut—an excellent roomy one—in which I enjoyed a pleasant night's rest after the fatigues of the day." Dr. Rae after this traced the W shore of Melville peninsula, forming probably the E side of the gulf of Boothia, as far as Cape Ellice, in N lat. 69° 42′, W long. 85° 8′, or to within a few miles of the W entrance of the strait of the Fury and Hecla. The remaining portion of the W coast of the peninsula of B., from Ross's furthest, and the whole of the N coast, remains yet to be traced. The animals seen on this peninsula were bears, deer, gluttons, foxes, wolves, white hares, seals, ptarmigan, snow-buntings, willow-partridges, and grouse. A heath with stems about an inch thick was the largest plant. The most interesting discovery made in connection with that of the peninsula of B., was the true position of the N magnetic pole on the SW coast, at the exact point where Professor Barlow's magnetic chart had shown that the curves of equal variation must meet. We must give Sir John Ross's account of this most important discovery in his own words, "The land at this place is very low near the coast, but it rises into ridges of fifty or sixty feet high about a mile inland. We could have wished that a place so important had possessed more of mark or note. It was scarcely censurable to regret that there was not a mountain to indicate a spot to which so much of interest must ever be attached; and I could even have pardoned any one among us who had been so romantic or absurd as to expect that the magnetic pole was an object as conspicuous and mysterious as the fabled mountain of Sinbad, that it even was a mountain of iron, or a magnet as large as Mont Blanc. But Nature had here erected no monument to denote the spot which she had chosen as the centre of one of her dark and great powers; and where we could do little ourselves towards this end, it was our business to submit, and to be content in noting by mathematical numbers and signs, as with things of far more importance in the terrestrial system, what we could but ill distinguish in any other manner. We were, however, fortunate in here finding some huts of Esquimaux, that had not long been abandoned. Unconscious of the value which not only we, but all the civilized world, attached to this place, it would have been a vain attempt on our part to account to them for our delight, had they been present. It was better for us that they were not; since we thus took possession of their works, and were thence enabled to establish our observations with the greater ease; encamping at six in the evening on a point of land about half-a-mile to the westward of those abandoned snow houses. The necessary observations were immediately commenced, and they were continued throughout this and the greater part of the following day. Of these, the details for the purposes of science have been since communicated to the Royal Society; as a paper containing all that philosophers require on the subject has now also been printed in their Transactions. I need not therefore repeat them here, even had it not been the plan of the whole of this volume to refer every scientific matter which had occurred to Captain Ross and myself to a separate work, under the name of an appendix. But it will gratify general curiosity to state the most conspicuous results in a simple and popular manner. The place of the observatory was as near to the magnetic pole as the limited means which I possessed enabled me to determine. The amount of the dip, as indicated by my dipping needle, was 89° 59', being thus within one minute of the vertical; while the proximity at least of this pole, if not its actual existence where we stood, was further confirmed by the action, or rather by the total inaction of the several horizontal needles then in my possession. These were suspended in the most delicate manner possible, but there was not one which showed the slightest effort to move from the position in which it was placed: a fact, which even the most moderately informed of readers must now know to be one which proves that the centre of attraction lies at a very small horizontal distance, if at any. As soon as I had satisfied my own mind on this subject, I made known to the party this gratifying result of all our joint labours; and it was then, that amidst mutual congratulations, we fixed the British flag on the spot, and took possession of the North magnetic pole and its adjoining territory, in the name of Great Britain and King William the Fourth. We had abundance of materials for building, in the fragments of limestone that covered the beach; and we therefore erected a cairn of some magnitude, under which we buried a canister, containing a record of the interesting fact: only regretting that we had not the means of constructing a pyramid of more importance, and of strength sufficient to withstand the assaults of time and of the Esquimaux. Had it been a pyramid as large as that of Cheops, I am not quite sure that it would have done more than satisfy our ambition, under the feelings of that exciting day. The lat. of this spot is 70° 5′ 17", and its long. 96° 46' 45" W."
BOOTHS (HIGHER and LOWER), two townships in Lancashire, the former 41 m., the latter 31 m., S by W of Burnley. Area of Higher B. 2,000 acres. BOQ Pop. 3,652. Area of Lower B. 630 acres. Pop. 2,464.

BOOTLE, a parish and town in Cumberland, 5 m. SSW of Ravenglass, on the coast of the Irish sea. Area of p. 6,570 acres. Pop. 696. The town consists of a single well-built street. It is a pollingplace for the members for W Cumberland; and trades with Liverpool in corn, pork, and bacon.
BOOTLE-WITH-LINACRE, a township and village in the p. of Walton-on-the-Hill, 3 m. N of Liverpool. Area of t. 1,050 acres. Pop. 1,962. The v. is much resorted to for bathing from Liverpool.
BOOTON, a parish in Norfolkshire, 5 m. SW of Aylsham. Area 1,030 acres. Pop. 241.

BOPAL, or BHOPAUL, a small Mahommedan principality in Central India; bounded on the E and S by the Bengal ceded districts of the Nerbudda, and on the W and N by the territories of Sindia. It lies between the N bank of the Nerbudda river, and the parallel of 23° 50'. Its length from E to W is about 130 m.; from N to S, 60 m. Its area is about 5,600 sq. m. The Vindhyan mountain-chain intersects its S portion in a course nearly parallel to the Nerbudda, and throughout several low spurs towards the central part of the prov. The Betwah, rising on the N slope of the Vindhyan chain, flows N, across the principality to the Jumna, passing 16 m. to the E of the town of B. Numerous smaller streams intersect the district, flowing generally through fertile valleys. Sugar, tobacco, cotton, and ginger are cultivated, in addition to all the different grains known in Central India. In 1820 the principality contained 4,130 villages; but 714 of them were reported as being uninhabited. The revenue of the rajah probably amounts to 30 lacs. The chief towns beside the cap. are Ashta, 40 m. SW, and Islamnagur, 5 m. N of B.—This small state was conferred by Aurungzebe on a Patan or Afghan adventurer, in 1723. It was the first of the powers of Central India which espoused the British cause. In 1813, its cap. was unsuccessfully besieged by Sindia and the rajah of Nagpur. In 1815, at the earnest solicitation of the nuwab, it was received under British protection; and the Company marked its sense of the uniform fidelity and attachment of this small state, by adding to it two very valuable districts of 867 villages. Several civil revolutions have been effected since 1827 in its internal politics; but none of these appear to have changed its relations to the Company's government.

BOPAL, the cap. of the above state, is in N lat. 23° 17', E long. 77° 28', 100 m. E of Oojein. It is surrounded by a stone wall with square towers; but has not recovered from the effects of its siege in 1813, when the besieged, though only 8,000 or 10,000 strong, resisted an assailing force of some 50,000 or 60,000. On a rising ground to the SW is a fort, under the walls of which is an extensive tank, or pond, about 6 m. in length, in which several small streams take their rise.
BOPFINGEN, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of Jaxt, bailiwick and 7 m. N of Neresheim, on the Eger. Pop. 1,500, chiefly Protestants. Woollen stuffs and leather are manufactured here.

BOPPARD, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, gov. and 9 m. S of Coblentz, on the I. bank of the Rhine. Pop. in 1837, 3,445. Cotton, tobacco, and leather are manufactured here, and the town carries on some trade by the Rhine.

BOQUEIRAO, a parish and hamlet of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, district of Geremoabo, near the source of the Rio Verde, an affluent of the Rio San Francisco.—Also a p. in the prov. of San-Pedro-do-Rio- Grande, in the district of Pelotas, to the S of the Camacuan river, and E of the Dos Patos lake.—Also a village in the prov. of Parahiba, on the Pianco river.—Also a serra in the prov. of Bahia, comarca of Centocé, in which the Rio Verde takes its rise.— Also a small circular islet off the N coast of the island of Governador, in the bay of Nitherohi, prov. of Rio Janeiro.

BOQUEIRAO - DO - TAQUARI, an extensive meadow formed on the banks of the Taquari river in Brazil, when the river has retired within its banks after the annual inundations. It commences 25 leagues below Ponzo-Alegre, and stretches to near the confluence of the Taquari with the Paraguay.
BOQUERONES POINT, a salient, high, and scarped head-land on the coast of Panama, in about 79° 42' W long. There are five islets called the B. bearing from it N 64° E.
BOQUILLAS (LAS), a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Chihuahua, 120 m. N by W of Chihuahua.
BOQUILLAS. See Boccas Cerradas.

BOR, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the prov. of Karamania, 8 m. SSW of Nigdeh, at the base of a ridge of hills at the NE extremity of the plain of Bor, and on the r. bank of a stream which intersects the plain and flows SW.

BORA, a river of Abyssinia, which rises in springs near Agamna, and some small sources further to the N, and flows S to the Abai.—Also a name given to a part of the Pindus range in Romelia, on the NW frontier of the sanjak of Monastir, uniting on the N with Mount Karopnitz, and on the S with Mount Magna-Petrinia. See also VITZI.
BORABORA. See BOLABOLA.
BORAJA, a town of Austria, in Dalmatia, 18 m. NW of Spalatro.

BORANDU, a village of Bambarra, in Central Africa, between Cacaron and Tangrera, containing about 400 or 500 inhabitants. The huts are chiefly built of earth, and have terraced roofs. Water is found here 10 or 12 ft. below the surface level.— Caillie.

BORAES, or Borahas, a town of Sweden, in the district and 52 m. SE of Wenersborg, and 40 m. E of Gottenburg, on the Viskee. Pop. in 1810, 1,750; in 1833, 2,328. It possesses manufactories of, and an active itinerant home trade in, woollen fabrics, and hardware, and dye-works; and has an extensive traffic in horses and cattle. The environs are wellwooded, and contain some mineral springs.
